---
title: "Harmonic Analysis 03-29-19"
output: 
  html_document: 
    toc: false
    toc_float: true
    theme: cosmo
    css: style.css
---

\newcommand{\C}{\mathbb{C}}
\newcommand{\Z}{\mathbb{Z}}
\newcommand{\N}{\mathbb{N}}
\newcommand{\R}{\mathbb{R}}
\newcommand{\<}{\left<}
\newcommand{\>}{\right>}
\newcommand{\ra}{\rightarrow}
\newcommand{\sinc}{\mathrm{sinc}}
\newcommand{\supp}{\mathrm{supp}}

## Wavelets: Multiresolution analysis (MRA)

Recall __Def:__ $\psi$ is a wavelet if $\{\psi_{ij}(x)\}=\{2^{j/2}\psi(2^jx-k)\}$ is an ONB of $L^2(\R)$.

An MRA is a general framework for constructing functions $\psi$ that form wavelets.

A multiresolution analysis consists of a sequence $\{V_j\}_{j\in\Z}$ of closed subspaces of $L^2(\R)$ and a function $\varphi\in V_0$, satisfying 

  i. $\dots\subset V_{-1}\subset V_0\subset V_1\subset \dots$
  
  ii. $\overline{\cup_j V_j}=L^2(\R)$ and $\cap_j V_j=\{0\}$
  
  iii. $f\in V_j$ iff $f(2x)\in V_{j+1}$ (which is the same as $f(2^{-j}x)\in V_0$)
  
  iv. $f\in V_0$ iff $f(x-k)\in V_0$ for each $k\in \Z$
  
  v. $\{\phi(x-k)\}_{k\in\Z}$ is an ONB of $V_0$.
  
Property iii. is known as the _Dilation-by-two feature_. This and property iv. are the key properties for constructing wavelets. Combining these properties, we see that if $f\in V_j$, then $\{f(x-k)\}\subset V_j$ and $\{f(2x),f(2x-k)\}\subset V_{j+1}$.

__Remark:__ Take $f\in L^2$. An approximation of $f$ at _level_ $j$ is in $V_j$. The $V_j$'s are called _approximation spaces_. $\phi$ is called a _scaling function_.

__Example:__ $I_{jk}=[2^{-j}k,2^{-j}(k+1))$

$$V_j=\{f\in L^2:\exists \ c\in\R \text{ s.t. } f|_{I_{jk}}=c \ \forall k\in \Z\}$$

  - these are the piecewise constant functions
  - this is just approximation by simple functions
  - associated with the Haar wavelet: $\mathrm{supp} \ \psi_{jk}\subset I_{jk}$
  - $\varphi(x)=\chi_{_{[0,1)}}(x)\implies \psi(x)=\varphi(2x)-\varphi(2x-1)$
  
$V_{j+1}$ has more detail than $V_j$. The difference is denoted by $W_j$. That is,

$$V_{j+1}=V_j\oplus W_j=\{v_j+w_j:v_j\in V_j, w_j\in W_j, v_j\perp w_j\}$$

Think of $v_j$ the orthogonal projection of $f$ onto $V_j$. What's left over is $w_j$. Thus, we can write

$$V_k=V_l\oplus W_l\oplus\dots\oplus W_{k-1}$$

for $l<k\in\Z$. In particular, $L^2=\bigoplus_j W_j$. The scaling function $\varphi$ generates a basis for $V_j$. If $\psi$ is the wavelet for $V_j$, then the $W_j$'s are generated by $\psi$.

  - $\varphi$ determines the approximation (the $V_j$'s)
  - the wavelets determine the "details" (the differences between $V_j$'s)
